coredata.dc:title: Green emitting N, P-doped carbon dots as efficient fluorescent nanoprobes for determination of Cr(VI) in water and soil samples

coredata.dc:description: The doping of carbon dots (CDs) with non-metallic elements has been a strategy to improve their optical properties and application fields. In the present work, nitrogen and phosphorus-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) were prepared through one-pot hydrothermal synthesis using phytic acid (PA) and L-arginine (Arg) as precursors. The synthesized N, P-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, FTIR, Raman, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopies. The N, P-CDs exhibited green fluorescence (λem = 515 nm) that can be quenched sensitively and selectively in the presence of Cr(VI), combining static quenching, electron transfer, and an inner filter effect. Consequently, a new fluorescence sensing method applied to Cr(VI) determination was developed using the presently synthesized fluorescent CDs. Under optimized conditions, a linear range from 0.05 to 3 µM in Cr(VI)) was obtained, with a limit of detection of 26 nM. Additionally, the N, P-CDs were selective toward Cr(VI) in the presence of Cr(III) and twenty other species, indicating its potential use to monitor Cr(VI) in environmental samples. Then, Cr(VI) was successfully determined, with recoveries from 85 to 107% in water and soil samples. The method, combining high sensitivity and selectivity, is easy to implement and promotes the use and valorization of eco-friendly materials.

Optimization of analytical parameters: (A) Effect of pH in Britton Robinson buffer (BR), 0.04 M [insert indicates ΔF = F0 - F of the N, P-CDs vs. pH]; (B) Effect of the buffer type [(1) = acetate, (2) = Citrate, (3) Citrate/Phosphate and (4) = BR]; Cbuffer = 0.04 M, pH 4; (C) Effect of the acetate buffer concentration and (D) Photostability and kinetics [inset indicates the quenching ratio (F0/F) vs. the time].

(1) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) solution; (2) excitation and (3) emission spectra of N, P-CDs; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) N, P-CDs; (b) N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) and (c) Cr(VI) solutions; (d) represents the difference of (b) and (c) spectra; (C) fluorescence decay of N, P-CDs (i); N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) 0.3 µM (ii) and N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) 3 µM (iii), conditions: λex / λem = 454 / 540 nm.

(B) Tolerance of foreign ions in the determination of 1 µM Cr(VI): Al(III), Fe(II), and Cu(II) were at 5 µM; the other ions at 25 µM.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The doping of carbon dots (CDs) with non-metallic elements has been a strategy to improve their optical properties and application fields. In the present work, nitrogen and phosphorus-doped CDs (N, P-CDs) were prepared through one-pot hydrothermal synthesis using phytic acid (PA) and L-arginine (Arg) as precursors. The synthesized N, P-CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy, FTIR, Raman, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopies. The N, P-CDs exhibited green fluorescence (λem = 515 nm) that can be quenched sensitively and selectively in the presence of Cr(VI), combining static quenching, electron transfer, and an inner filter effect. Consequently, a new fluorescence sensing method applied to Cr(VI) determination was developed using the presently synthesized fluorescent CDs. Under optimized conditions, a linear range from 0.05 to 3 µM in Cr(VI)) was obtained, with a limit of detection of 26 nM. Additionally, the N, P-CDs were selective toward Cr(VI) in the presence of Cr(III) and twenty other species, indicating its potential use to monitor Cr(VI) in environmental samples. Then, Cr(VI) was successfully determined, with recoveries from 85 to 107% in water and soil samples. The method, combining high sensitivity and selectivity, is easy to implement and promotes the use and valorization of eco-friendly materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Chromium (Cr) is a metallic element that has been used in various industrial processes in its different chemical forms, such as electroplating, dyes’ syntheses, and leather tanning, due to its versatile chemical and physical properties [1–3]. However, the chromium generated and discharged to the environment from those processes may be highly toxic to living organisms, especially in its hexavalent form, which has been associated with mutagenic and carcinogenic effects, affecting public health and the environment [4,5]. In this sense, the World Health Organization (WHO) has set a maximum level of Cr(VI) in drinking water at 50 µg L−1 [6]. As such, sensitive and selective methods are essential for the monitoring of this species in environmental samples. Atomic spectroscopic [7,8], chromatographic [9,10], and electrochemical [11] methods have been reported as suitable for the determination of Cr(VI) in various samples. However, most of these methods require relatively complex instrumentations, along with time-consuming sample processing. Additionally, most of these methods are not adequate in distinguishing Cr(VI) from Cr(III). Therefore, simple, fast, sensitive, and selective methods are desirable for the monitoring of Cr(VI) in environmental samples. In this context, molecular fluorescence has been reported to be a suitable tool for detecting various pollutants, including chromium, due to the advantages of easy operation, high sensitivity, and the use of a plethora of fluorescent nanoprobes, especially those based on carbon dots (CDs).

CDs are the latest family of carbon-based nanomaterials that display a core containing sp2 conjugated carbons with surface defects (typically sp3 hybridized carbons). They are modified with various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, sulfhydryl, amine, and amides, which favor their solubility in polar and mixed aqueous/organic solvents. Compared to the traditional fluorescent organic dyes and semiconductors quantum dots, CDs offer high photostability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and wide analytical applications [12–16]. Also, given the numerous carbonaceous starting materials combined with the various synthetic routes, CDs with different optical properties can be produced, which makes their exploration almost inexhaustible. Doping/co-doping with heteroatoms is still strategic to produce CDs with high photoluminescence quantum yield and surface binding sites. Nitrogen, sulfur, boron, and phosphorus doping revealed a marked influence on fluorescent carbonaceous nanoparticles' structural and optical properties [17–20]. Within the various synthetic approaches, hydrothermal heating has become widely used due to its simplicity, eco-friendly and economical features [21].

CDs have been used mainly in fluorescence sensing for several analytes, including Cr(VI), due to their attractive characteristics. Pingjing et al. [22] produced a water-soluble N-doped CD through the calcination of diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid that has been further used for the determination of Cr(VI) with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.15 µM. Carbon dots with high photoluminescence have been prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid and reduced glutathione and used as efficient nanoprobes for Cr(VI) sensing in water and soil samples, based on fluorescence quenching and inner filter effect processes [23]. Recently, Meng et al. [24] reported a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped CDs, using ammonia and p-phenylenediamine as starting materials. The as-synthesized materials served as fluorescent nanoprobes for the detection of Cr(VI) and bio-imaging. Sing et al. [25] reported N and P dual doped CDs' production, using green precursor (biomass of halophilic microalgae Dunaliella Salina). The produced CDs with blue fluorescence were used as a sensor for the determination of Hg(II) and Cr(VI) with a limit of detection of 0.018 µM for Cr(VI). Phosphorus doped CDs have been reported, most often using phosphoric acid as a P source. However, Wang et al. [26] reported N, P-doped CDs with green fluorescence through the microwave treatment of PA as a P and C-rich compound and ethylenediamine (EDA) a N source. Later, Lingli et al. [27] reproduced the same procedure, using the same precursors to synthesize N, P-doped CDs with green fluorescence, also used in fluorescence sensing of Cr(VI), in aqueous media with a LOD of 0.24 µM. Then, PA appears to be an efficient precursor in P-doped CDs' production through the microwave route. On the other hand, it is desirable to substitute EDA by an eco-friendly N source. In this way, Arg, as an amino acid with high N content, seems to be a promising alternative

Thus, in this work, PA and Arg, as environmentally friendly precursors, were treated hydrothermally and N, P-CDs were successfully produced and characterized. Further, the synthesized materials showed high sensitivity and selectivity toward Cr(VI) based on fluorescence quenching associated with redox reaction, interaction process, and inner filter effect. As a consequence, an efficient method for Cr(VI) determination in water and soil samples was developed.

All the reagents used in the experiments are of analytical grade purity, and no further purifications were performed before their use. The precursors in the synthesis of the N, P-CDs were PA solution (50% m/v, Sigma-Aldrich) and L-Arg (Vetec). Ultrapure type II water (electrical conductivity lower than 1.0 µS cm−1) was used during the synthesis and preparation of all the solutions. A 5 mL stock solution of Cr(VI) 0.01 M was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of K2Cr2O7 (Merck) in water, and adequate dilutions were made to obtain the working solutions. The Britton-Robinson buffers (pH 2 to 12) were prepared by mixing H3PO4 (Sigma-Aldrich), H3BO3 (Merck), and acetic acid (Sigma-Aldrich), all at 0.04 M. The final pH was adjusted with NaOH or HCl 1 M. Acetate, citrate, and citrate/phosphate buffers (all at 0.04 M) were prepared and used in the optimization of the buffer system. In determining the quantum yield, a 0.1 M quinine sulfate solution was prepared by dissolving the adequate amount of quinine hemisulfate monohydrate (Sigma-Aldrich). A solution of Na2CO3 0.1 M (Merck) was used for the selective extraction of Cr(VI) from soil samples.

PA (1 mL; 2.17 mmol) and Arg (17.42 mg; 1 mmol) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water with mild stirring until a homogenous mixture was obtained. The resulting solution was then transferred into a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless autoclave and the whole heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Next, the darkish brown reaction mixture was allowed to cool naturally at room temperature. For the purification of the nanoparticles, 1.0 mL of the raw mixture was diluted with 5 mL of acetone and subjected to magnetic stirring for 6 h. The resulting solution was further centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. Then, the upper solution was collected and evaporated at 60 °C, using a rotating evaporator. Finally, the viscous material was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water and filtered through a PVDF membrane (Micropore) with 0.22 µm pores sizes.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a dual-beam spectrophotometer with a photodiode type detector (AJX-6100PC, Micronal, Brazil). The measurements were carried out in the 200 to 800 nm region, using water as a reference in a 10 mm optical pathway quartz cells. The emission spectra were obtained on a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). The spectra were recorded in the 320–650 nm range in 10 mm optical pathway quartz cells, using excitation wavelengths from 300 to 500 nm. A 150 W xenon lamp was used as the excitation source. Both excitation and emission slits width were set at 5 nm. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements were carried out using a Microtrack (Zetatrack, 5 mW solid-state diode lasers λ, 780 nm, USA). The zeta potentials (ζ) were measured using a NanoBrook Omni Particle Analyzer (Brookhaven Instruments, USA).

The N, P-CDs' surface morphology was determined using a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 200 kV. The sample was dispersed in water ultrasonically for 15 min, deposited onto a carbon film, and left to dry in the procedure. Next, the carbon film was placed onto a mica sheet that was further deposited onto the Cu grids (400 mesh; 3 mm diameter).

The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet IR200 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) infrared spectrophotometer using potassium bromide (KBr) as a dispersing agent. Initially, the samples and KBr were weighed to make the tablets. The following operational parameters were used: the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1, resulting in an average scan of 65 scans in transmittance mode, with a resolution of 4 cm−1.

The Raman spectra of the produced N, P-CDs were collected using a confocal fluorescence microscope (LabRam HR Evolution, HORIBA) equipped with a detector synapse, a cooled CCD detector. All measurements were conducted using a 100X (NA = 0.90) objective, which focused the 532 nm C.W. diode laser on the sample with a spot size of ca. 2 μm. After interaction with the sample, the subsequent Raman scattering light was collected by the same objective in the backscattering geometry and dispersed by 1,800 grooves mm−1. The acquisition time was 8 s (with 50 s of accumulation time) for each spectrum.

The fluorescence lifetime (τ) measurements were performed using a NanoLogTM Fluorimeter (HORIBA) armed with a Xenon flash lamp as the excitation source. The excitation wavelength was set at 454 nm, and the fluorescence emission was monitored at 540 nm. The fluorescence light emitted by the samples was transferred to a monochromator equipped with a diffraction grating Horiba with 600 grooves mm−1 and blaze at 1000 nm, for the selection of specific wavelengths, and then detected by using a photomultiplier detector (model R928P), under the TCSPC (Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting) configuration. All the lifetime measurements were carried out at room temperature and with the same instrumental parameters. The experimental lifetime decay curves were best following a monoexponential fit according to the Eq. (1):

IF(t) and IF0 indicates the fluorescence intensity at a specific time t and at time zero for N, P-CDs, respectively.

The quantum yield (QY) of the N, P-CDs was determined by a comparative method, using quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54%) as a reference [23]. The QY was calculated as follows:

where the subscripts “s” and “ref” stand for N, P-CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively; “m” represents the slope from the linear regression of the graph integrated fluorescence intensity vs. absorbance and “η” the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 in this case). The inner filter effect was minimized using solutions with absorption intensities below 0.1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the fluorescence detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para:
The Cr(VI) determination using the N, P-CDs was performed in acetate buffer 0.05 M (pH 4). Typically, 40 µL of the N, P-CDs were mixed with the buffer system in the presence of adequate aliquots of the analyte to reach final concentrations in the range 0.05–3 µM (2.6–156 μg L−1 in Cr), the final volume being fixed at 2 mL. After 10 min of reaction/interaction time, the fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range 500–650 nm using an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity at 515 nm was used in the optimization steps as well as for the quantitative analysis of Cr(VI). The analytical parameters were evaluated using the slope of the Eq. (3):
(3)
F 0
F
= a
C
C r
VI
+ b

In which F0 and F represent the respective fluorescence intensities of the N, P-CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), at a given concentration (CCr(VI) ). The linear fit's slope and the linear coefficient are represented by a and b , in this order. All analytical curves consisted of at least five points (n = 5) with triplicate measurements (n = 3). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on the three times the standard deviation (LOD = 3 × sd/ a ), where sd represents the standard deviation for ten independent measurements of the blank signal, and a is the slope of the analytical curve. The method's precision was evaluated based on the relative standard deviation (RSD) of ten independent measurements (n = 10) for a given standard solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was used to analyze different kinds of water and soil samples. Water samples consisted of laboratory tap water, drinking water, river, and estuarine water, all from the city of Maceió (Alagoas, Brazil). The collected samples were initially filtered through a 0.22 µm pore size cellulose membrane and then spiked with three levels of Cr(VI) before measurements. Three soil samples (clayey, peat, and lettuce cultivation soils) were collected and characterized. Then, for the selective extraction of Cr(VI) in these soils, the procedure reported by Panichev et al. (2003) [28] was employed.

The optical properties of the synthesized N, P-CDs were investigated based on UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopies. The N, P-CDs exhibit a broad absorption from the UV region until the visible spectrum (Fig. 1 A). The absorption band at 269 nm could be assigned to the π → π* transitions (from the carbon core), whereas those at 292 and 344 nm may originate from the n → π* transitions related to the surface states/defects induced by N, O and P heteroatoms. These observations in the UV region of the spectra follow CDs' traditional UV–vis characteristics [27,29,30]. The broad peak observed at about 450 nm may originate from amino or phosphate groups at the surface of the produced CD [31,32]. Thus, from the UV–vis characteristics, the formation of an sp2 carbon core with surface groups consisting of carbonyl/carboxyl, amino, and phosphate may have been achieved [29]. The excitation spectrum in the visible region (485 nm) indicates that the fluorescence emission at 515 nm is caused by the surface states of the N, P-CDs [33].

The molecular fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1B) indicate that N, P-CDs exhibit two emission bands, depending on the excitation wavelengths. From 310 to 380 nm, the N, P-CDs emit at about 420 nm, with an excitation-dependent emission profile. Meanwhile, longer excitation wavelengths (400 to 500 nm) lead to the green color emission located at 515 nm, with an almost excitation-independent emission behavior in this interval, with the highest fluorescence intensity, obtained under an excitation wavelength of 480 nm. This behavior can be better observed by plotting the emission wavelength variation concerning the excitation energy (Fig. S1, supplementary material). The fluorescence emission characteristics agree with previously reported works in which PA was used as a P source [26,27]. Furthermore, the bands centered at 515 nm are narrower than those observed in the 310–380 nm excitation range, suggesting a control over the surface states or emission centers of the N, P-CDs, as previously suggested by Rajneesh et al. [34]. The blue (420 nm) and green (515 nm) emissions indicate the presence of two electronic transitions process involving the core structure and surface states of the CDs produced [35].

The fluorescence quantum yield of the N, P-CDs was estimated, using quinine sulfate as reference. The integrated fluorescence intensities of both the reference and the N, P-CDs samples were plotted against their respective absorbance (Fig. S2). The calculated QY is as low as 1.8%, which is similar to that of reported P-rich CDs (3.5%) synthesized hydrothermally [36] and smaller than the QY of P-rich CDs (19%) obtained through microwave treatment [26]. Moreover, the low QY value could be related to the excitation energy used (360 nm) being relatively far from the excitation wavelength of the N, P-CDs (485 nm, Fig. 1A). Besides, some N and P doped CDs with relatively low QY (8% [25], 15% [37]) were already reported.

The FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the surface functional groups of the N, P-CDs. The results showed a strong and broadband centered at 3550 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds [38] (Fig. 1C). The peak at 1785 cm−1 is associated with C=O stretching vibrations, while the one at 1535 cm−1 could result from the stretching vibrations of N–H and C=C [27]. Other characteristics bands appearing at 1277, 1100, 965, and 546 cm−1 are ascribed respectively to the stretching vibration of P=O from the phosphate groups, H-bonded P=O, P–O–R (R = aromatic ring) and –PO4 3− [26,27,35,39]. The FTIR spectroscopy investigations indicate that the N, P-CDs have abundant functional groups, that enhance their water solubility and stability [40]. Based on the peaks observed, along with the UV–vis results, one may suppose the production of N, P co-doped CDs during the hydrothermal treatment of the precursors [41].

In the Raman spectrum of N, P-CDs (Fig. 1D), it was possible to distinguish the bands located at 1406 and 1603 cm−1 assigned to the respective disordered D and ordered G bands [42]. The ratio of the intensities of the D to G bands (ID/IG ) is frequently used to evaluate the degree of graphitization of carbon-based nanomaterials. For N, P-CDs, this ratio is estimated at 0.97, indicating the predominance of ordered graphitic sp2 carbon atoms compared to surface state sp3 carbon atoms [42,43]. Besides, the Raman spectrum of the N, P-CDs displays a peak at 1518 cm−1 that is relative to aromatic carbon. Such a peak has already been observed by the group of Cheng et al. [44].

The surface morphology structure of the produced CDs revealed by TEM (Fig. 1E) indicate well dispersed and quasi-spherical nanoparticles with narrow size distribution (2.46 to 5.95 nm), with an average diameter of 3.14 ± 0.46 nm (inset Fig. 1E). The HRTEM (Fig. 1F) revealed a crystal lattice spacing of 0.19 nm, which is ascribed to (102) lattice planes of sp2 graphitic carbon [27], following the observations based on the Raman spectroscopy results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N, P-CDs in Cr(VI) sensing
ce:section.ce:para: As revealed by the fluorescence spectroscopy experiments, the N, P-CDs exhibited two emission bands located at 420 and 515 nm. However, the large band at 420 nm showed weak fluorescence intensity compared to the band at 515 nm. As such, the more intense and well-resolved band at 515 nm was chosen for further quenching assays. Then, a set of metallic ions at the same concentration (5 µM) were evaluated on their influence on the N, P-CDs' fluorescence intensity, and Cr(VI) was found to have higher quenching efficiency, the fluorescence being almost totally suppressed at this concentration of Cr(VI). Then, analytical parameters were optimized to yield the best conditions for Cr(VI) sensing.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the N, P-CDs in Britton Robinson buffer at pH from 2 to 12 were recorded. It was found that the nanoparticles exhibit higher fluorescence intensity in an acidic medium (pH 4), followed by decreasing fluorescence intensities in both directions on the pH scale (Fig. 2 A). However, acceptable fluorescence intensities were obtained from pH 5 to 8, relatively to the highest intensity at pH 4. The greater fluorescence intensity (pH 4) could be explained by the stability of the surface groups [35,45], which are protonated, providing a high net surface charge that makes the N, P-CDs more hydrophilic with enhanced stability and dispersibility features in water [46]. Extreme acidic and basic media yielded weak fluorescence intensities that may originate from the emitting sites' damage [26]. Besides, it was found that the higher quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 4, when the fluorescence of the N, P-CDs was recorded in the presence of Cr(VI), at different pH values (inset Fig. 2A). The variation in quenching efficiency may originate from the probe's surface charges (through acid-base equilibrium) and the predominant form of the analyte, in the solution through the equilibrium: 2CrO4 2− + 2H+
⇌ Cr2O7 2− + H2O. At pH 4, the Cr(VI) species are predominantly in dichromate form (Cr2O7 2−) which may have electrostatic interactions with the charged surface groups of the probe from positively amine groups, or by hydrogen bonding, since the FTIR indicates the presence of carboxylic and phosphate groups that when in an acid medium (pH = 4) are protonated. In the same way, when increasing the pH values, the carbon dots bear increasing negative charge owing to deprotonation of surface groups, which disfavors interactions with the negatively charged predominant specie of Cr(VI), the yellow chromate ion (CrO4 2−) in these conditions [23]. Based on these results, the pH 4 value was selected, and different buffer systems were evaluated.

The analytical sensitivities were then evaluated in acetate, citrate, citrate/phosphate, and Britton Robinson buffers (Fig. 2B). The higher sensitivity was achieved with the acetate buffer when compared to the other systems. This observation indicates that the acetate buffer at pH 4 favors better interactions between the probe and the analyte. Citrate/phosphate and Britton Robinson buffers could interact with the Cr(VI) due to their phosphate groups, while citrate buffer may induce a reduction of Cr(VI) to the trivalent form, which explains the lower sensitivities observed with these buffers [47,48]. As acetate buffer was defined as the best buffer system, its concentration was further optimized, and an optimum value of 50 mM was chosen, based on the sensitivity (Fig. 2C). The volume of the N, P-CDs suspension was further optimized, as it can influence the sensitivity. In a typical experiment, the analytical sensitivity was evaluated for the N, P-CDs volumes of 20, 30, 40, and 60 µL. It was found that higher sensitivity and linearity were obtained with a volume of 40 µL (Fig. S3).

The photostability of the carbon dots and the quenching process's kinetics were evaluated under the previously optimized conditions. The carbon dots' fluorescence intensity diminished notably under constant irradiation for 1 h (about 50% of the initial fluorescence suppressed), indicating a relatively poor photostability of the materials (Fig. 2D), possibly associated with oxidation process on N, P-CDs surface. It is well known that CDs, in general, possess high photostability; however, Wang et al. (2016) [49] reported that CDs derived from citric acid and urea have enhanced photobleaching as a consequence of photochemical process related to the surface status of the CDs. In the presence of Cr(VI), the fluorescence intensity displays the same behavior, showing almost instantaneous interactions between CDs and the analyte. A higher quenching rate was obtained a few minutes upon Cr(VI) addition to the N, P-CDs system (inset of Fig. 2D). Based on these results, the system N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) was incubated for ≈ 10 min before recording the spectra in future measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the quenching and interaction mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching process generally can occur through the inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, or dynamic quenching. IFE quenching process requires a spectral overlap of the CDs excitation and/or emission spectra and the absorbance spectrum of the analyte. Consequently, the analyte can partially shield the excitation energy or absorb the emitted light from the CDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching [50,51]. Static quenching is related to forming a non-fluorescent ground-state complex involving the probe and the analyte, while dynamic quenching occurs when the CDs in their excited state interact with the analyte through energy charge transfer [52].

The Cr(VI) solution's absorption spectrum was then recorded, together with the excitation and emission spectra of the N, P-CDs. The Cr(VI) solutions presented absorption bands at 259, 349, and 448 nm, while the N, P-CDs display maximum excitation/emission at 485 and 513 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 A). A partial overlap between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) solution and the N, P-CDs excitation spectrum can be observed. In this way, Cr(VI) partially shields the probe's excitation light, which indicates that the IFE is likely participating in the quenching mechanism, as in previous reports [53,54].

The absorption spectra of the N, P-CDs, Cr(VI), and the N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) systems were recorded to evaluate the possible participation of dynamic or static quenching. Indeed, with the dynamic quenching process, the mentioned systems' spectra should not show absorption wavelength variation, in contrast to the static quenching process. The maximal absorption wavelength of the N, P-CDs (curve a) is red-shifted from 450 to 505 nm upon addition of Cr(VI) (curve b), which suggests an N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) ground state complex formation (Fig. 3B). Thus, a static quenching mechanism occurs in the fluorescence quenching process [50]. Moreover, when the spectrum of Cr(VI) (curve c) was suppressed to the spectrum of the (N, P-CDs)-Cr(VI) system (curve b), the resulting spectrum (curve d) doesn't match the one of N, P-CDs (Fig. 3B). The resulting curve d exhibits higher absorbance intensity and longer absorption wavelength when compared to the original spectra. The higher absorbance observed reinforces the hypothesis of the inner filter effect [27,55]. Thus, the results suggest a combination of IFE and static quenching mechanisms.

In the quenching mechanism investigation, additional experiments concerning temperature effects and time-resolved fluorescence lifetime were performed. The quenching efficiency was then evaluated at different temperatures (303, 310, and 317 K) and the Stern Volmer and binding constants (K SV and K b, respectively) were calculated from the following equations:
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
×
Cr(VI)
(5)
log
F 0
- F
F
= log
K b
+ n × log
Cr ( V I )

In which F 0 and F are the respective fluorescence intensities of the N, P-CDs in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), K SV the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, K b is the binding constant, n is the number of binding sites, and [Cr(VI)] is the molar concentration of quencher. The K SV and K b values decreased with the temperature elevation (Table 1 ), which are related to disfavored interaction processes. Thus, it is suggested that the static quenching process occurs with the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex [23], once, with temperature elevation, the increase of molecular collisions impair the complex formation, resulting in a decrease of the Ksv and K b parameters. The time-resolved fluorescence lifetime showed an almost constant lifetime for the nanoprobes in the analyte's absence and presence (Fig. 3C). Indeed, the fluorescence lifetimes values were found to be 79.93, 72.82, and 77.07 ns for N, P-CDs, and N, P-CDs + Cr(VI) (0.3 and 3 µM, respectively), in this order. This lifetime value of N, P-CDs is, to the best of our knowledge, longer than those of the most reported CDs and may be related to the higher sp2 domain in the carbogenic core [56], as shown by Raman spectrum, already discussed. The practically constant lifetime (maximum variation of ≤ 4.7%) suggests a static quenching process, which is in line with the above thermodynamic and spectroscopic measurements.

In addition, to investigate further the mechanism of quenching N, P-CDs for Cr(VI), dynamic light scattering (DLS) of N, P-CDs without and with different Cr(VI) concentrations (5 and 50 µM) were measured (Fig. S4). The DLS profile reveals that the average size of N, P-CDs in the absence, and the presence of Cr(VI) has not changed to any significant extent, indicating that no aggregates are being formed, supporting the IFE and static quenching mechanism. Besides, the hydrodynamic size of N, P-CDs revealed by the DLS is similar to the particle size obtained in the TEM experiments. Thus, the quenching mechanism is a combination of IFE and static quenching. Further, the thermodynamic parameters ΔH and ΔS and the Gibbs' free energy (ΔG) were deduced from the Van't Hoff relation and equation (7):
(6)

(
K b
)

Δ H

Δ S
R
(7)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S
Where R stands for the universal gas constant, and T represents the temperature [23]. The equation's linear fit (6) with temperature variation (Fig. S5) was used to calculate ΔH and ΔS. Based on the results, it can be concluded that the interactions of Cr(VI) with the nanoprobes are spontaneous (ΔG = −0.89 kJ mol−1 < 0). Besides, it was found that the enthalpy factor (ΔH = −39.63 kJ mol−1) was predominant when compared to the entropic one (ΔS = −130 J K mol−1). These observations indicate van der Walls and hydrogen bonding interactions between the Cr(VI) ions and the nanoprobes [23,57]. Besides that, the ionic strength's influence was further evaluated to confirm the hypothesis (Fig. S6). It was found that the presence of NaCl up to 0.3 M in the (N, P-CDs)-Cr(VI) system didn't influence the sensitivity, from which it can be concluded that electrostatic interactions are not involved in the system. Then, the interactions between the Cr(VI) and the N, P-CDs are mainly of van der Walls and hydrogen bonds.

Furthermore, it has been reported that redox reactions can occur involving fluorescent CDs and analyte [37,58,59]. Thus, in this work, some experiments were conducted to investigate the possible redox reaction in the N, P-CDs-Cr(VI) system. First, the reversibility of the nanoprobes was assessed. The fluorescence spectrum of N, P-CDs in the presence of Cr(VI) was then recorded. To this system, the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) or hydroxylamine (HA), in stoichiometric proportions, could induce the reduction of the Cr(VI) ions to the trivalent form Cr(III), which would restore the fluorescence signal, once Cr(III) was not supposed to induce any quenching in these conditions. However, both AA or HA were not able to restore the initial fluorescence intensity of N, P-CDs, in the presence of Cr(VI) (Fig. S7); despite their favorable reduction potentials for the redox reaction Cr(VI) → Cr(III).

Based on these results, we speculated that Cr(VI) might have already been reduced to Cr(III) upon their interaction with the nanoprobes, as it has been already reported in other studies [37,59]. This data could have been caused by electron donors' presence on the surface of N, P-CDs, and the strong oxidizing capacity of Cr2O7 2− in acidic conditions. In this way, the zeta potential value (ζ) of the probe may have been changed upon the addition of Cr(VI); in particular, an increase of the charge is expected. Then, the ζ measurements of the nanoprobes, in the presence and the absence of Cr(VI), were performed to confirm this proposal. It was found that the N, P-CDs dispersion exhibits a positive ζ value (+0.81 ± 0.25 mV) (Table S1, supplementary material), which is consistent regarding the protonated surface groups of N, P-CDs, under the experimental conditions. Upon addition of Cr(VI) at 5 and 50 µM to the N, P-CDs system, the ζ values were increased to + 5.53 ± 2.09 and + 3.11 ± 1.71 mV, respectively. The increase in the ζ values, in comparison to the ζ of the N, P-CDs, is following the predicted theory. Thus, from these results, the Cr(VI) ions are likely to be reduced to Cr(III) in contact with N, P-CDs. Scheme 1 illustrates the quenching and interaction mechanisms between N, P-CDs, and Cr(VI).

The method's selectivity was evaluated in the presence of several potential interfering species in determining Cr(VI) by the proposed method. The results (Fig. 5 A) indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe was not significantly affected by 100 µM of Cr(III), Hg(II), Mg(II), Pb(II), Zn(II), Cd(II), Ca(II), Ba(II), As(III), Ag(I), I−, Br−; 50 µM of Cu(I), Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III) and 25 µM of Al(III), in the optimized conditions, considering the signal variation tolerance of ± 20% [65]. Thus, the nanoprobe showed good selectivity to Cr(VI) in the dynamic linear range and could be used to differentiate Cr(III) from Cr(VI).

Further, the tolerance of these species in the determination of Cr(VI) was evaluated. It was found that these species (in the 5–25 µM range) didn't show a significant effect on the fluorescence quenching when compared to the Cr(VI) alone (Fig. 5B), indicating the selectivity of the method. At higher levels, either negative and positive interferences were observed, which may be explained by the synergistic quenching effects (notably for Cu(I), Cu(II), Fe(II), Fe(III) and Al(III)), the formation of low soluble salts involving Cr(VI) species (with Ba(II) and Pb(II)). Finally, from these results, it can be concluded that the method could be used for environmental sample analysis without prior treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr(VI) in environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: Given the method's analytical performance, its practicability in determining Cr(VI) in real samples was further assessed. For this purpose, water and soil samples from different sources were collected, spiked with three levels of Cr(VI) (0.5, 1, and 2 µM), and subjected to recovery assays. The water samples were initially filtered through a 0.22 µM membrane pore size, and 0.1 M Na2CO3 was used for selective extraction of Cr(VI) in the soil samples [28]. The method showed the capability to recover the spiked amount of Cr(VI) in the water and soil samples; the recovery assays range from 85 to 107%, with an RSD lower than 6%, when considering the overall samples (Table 3 ). Thus, the method tends to be highly efficient and reliable for environmental sample analysis, considering the estuarine, river water, and soil samples' complexity.

A useful nitrogen and phosphorus co-doping of carbon dots were achieved through a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of PA and L-arginine. Moreover, the green fluorescence of the resulting N, P-CDs was selectively and sensitively quenched in the presence of the toxic Cr(VI) ions, which has made possible the development of a method for the determination of these ions in environmental samples. The method showed good selectivity and sensibility with a LOD estimated at 26 nM. The N, P-CDs were successfully used to recover Cr(VI) from water and soil samples, demonstrating their reliability and feasibility in the monitoring of Cr(VI) in these samples, along with their easy obtention from low-cost precursors.
